sentences.text: Yeah, OK, that's the-- the situation I was in-- in process to describe.
And I didn't finish, but maybe I will answer you after finishing this-- this episode.
So when she was-- when Liliana was at this apartment, and the cleaning lady was coming, sometimes she asked her son, a grown up son, to come and help her with-- to dispose something and so on.
So that's the-- the way how this son of that cleaning woman have seen Lillian, and he had the version, official version that is the-- the rich peasant, Polish peasant daughter hiding there.
When-- [SIGHS] when we were at this yard, and this new transport came from Warsaw, and this man was that son of that cleaning woman.
Of course, logically, we-- we knew something right away, following-- you know, that there is something fishy with her and her version while she was living in Warsaw.

And that's why Liliana said to me that we are lost because she suspected that maybe this boy will put 1 and 2 together-- and together, and will come to the conclusion that probably me or-- or her who are Jewish people, and therefore, we were in jeopardy.
So shortly speaking, after that, we decided to run away from that place, from the Kapfenberg.
Luckily, we had already some acquaintance, some Polish man, who was already working, but who came from Vienna.
And he had some connections in Vienna.
And, of course, not telling him the truth, only that we don't want to stay in Kapfenberg because we cannot get [SIGHS] some kind of-- of-- of housing together.
So we are just living and going to Vienna and looking for-- in Vienna for some solution for us.
And he even volunteered to-- to go with us together to Vienna because he had still, in Vienna, a lot of friends.
So anyway, this way we boarded the-- I don't remember, the next day or after two days, we went by train to Vienna.
And he was working before with a group of people-- men from Czechoslovakia and from Poland in a-- in the newspaper, a Vienna newspaper.
And they were quartering in a-- taken by the government, the German government, a-- a restaurant.
The owner of the restaurant was [SIGHS] in Army, and the whole restaurant was used as the dormitory for those workers of the newspaper.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because of-- of quite a lot of those workers were from Czechoslovakia, and from Vienna to Czechoslovakia is very close, so every weekend, they were going to-- to Bratislava [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to see their families.
And, of course, in this dormitory there were empty beds.
And in this way, this-- this guy, this Polish man, he-- he-- he knew everybody there, and he had the access.
I think he had the key, even.
So anyway, we-- the-- for-- through this weekend, we slept in this dormitory.
And after-- after-- after the weekend, we had to find out some place.
So we got a-- a room in a hotel in Vienna.
And interesting that because, well, we were always afraid that the-- maybe the police or something was looking for-- asking for our documents and so on.
So anyway, this hotel gave us the room without any problems, but of course, they-- they could not be responsible for our safety.
So anyway, one night, we were waked up of terrible noise.
And I thought, and Liliana thought-- thought too that somebody's banging the doors.
And he and I answer, where is that, and so on.
But apparently, that was in pipe-- pipes, in the-- you know, in the hotel, piping or plumbing.
They're also called-- there is this known effect.
There is this air backup.
So sometimes those pipes, they are making noise.
Apparently, there was nobody banging our doors, but there was the pipes.
So anyway, the next few days we-- we were looking for-- for occupation, for somewhere to work, because you cannot in Vienna to-- to be without work.

And this Polish man told us, because he was working there, too, that there is a company, this construction company of engineer Edward Meyer who had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] such a-- not a factory, works in-- in a 24th [INAUDIBLE], Vienna, which were-- and they were doing their bridges and such steel works.
So he gave us address and names and so on.
So I went to that engineer, Meyer, and he received me, and I asked to work for him.
And his-- his question, first question was, do I know static?
And I said, of course, I know static.
I learned in this [INAUDIBLE] school in Warsaw.
So he gave me a problem.
He said, OK.
Sit down here, and do the calculation how you will do the-- a small bridge over a-- a-- some ditch or something like that.
And he gave me some-- some data for it and a special book with the steel profiles.
And-- and he said, solve this problem.
Fortunately, I knew how to do it.
So I draw the Cremona diagram, and I calculated the forces and so on, and I looked in the book, and I find out that this proffer will be good for to carry this load and so on.
And he looked at that and said, very good.
You are hired.
So in this way, I got the job.
Until the end.
So after a day or two, I went there, this engineer, Meyer, asked her secretary, [SIGHS] Frau Fabianova, to arrange for us place in a-- in a camp for the-- for the volunteer workers.
All the frame-- all the foreign workers, they had to live in, the Poles were forbidden to live privately.
So OK, she arranged it.
She arranged in bribing the-- the-- the-- the camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] director or manager by giving him 200 cigarettes.
Because when coming-- when I was coming from Warsaw to Kapfenburg, I-- I took with-- with me a few cartons of cigarettes because cigarettes was like [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], like money.
So she gave 200, one carton of cigarettes to this guy and didn't ask any questions.
Because we didn't have the-- all the documents, they-- they were left in this company, this Danish company in Kapfenburg.
So we had no documents here.
So we got the-- the shelter, and I got the job, and I think two days after, I went to-- there was a manager, a different manager, because this engineer was living in a different place.
And [SIGHS] for our office, there was a-- a-- a special manager, and this manager was from Breslau, from Warsaw, now Polish city Warsaw.
And I-- I told him that with me is my wife, and I would like that she works in-- in the same office.
And she agreed, and she asked me to-- to tell her to come.
And Lillian was, well, such an office help.
She was going to the post office, they're going, you know, to-- to some offices to do for this boss and so and so.
And she was together with other two people.
There was a very pleasant, young, of the same age, Viennese girl and-- and then a man, an older but very funny, very, really interesting.
Well, older, he's not older.
He was-- he had to wear glasses, so therefore, he was not in the Army.
And we survived this-- the war in this office.
1943, yeah, still 1943.
Oh, that's a good question.
I think originally, I-- well, I had no answer.
Even when my sister met me, because I met-- when we came from Dubno, I looked for my sister, and she was working in Warsaw, and after 5 o'clock, she was walking to-- toward this apartment.
And I met her, and she was very surprised seeing me.
And her question was, what do you do?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
She was outraged because she heard already these-- these-- those rumors and so on.
And at that time she said, you get involved with a Jewish girl?
How come?
How-- how dare you do it?
So she was very surprised.
And apparently, since then, until the end of the war, I had no contact and no-- nothing with my parents and with her.
Because I couldn't trust them.
I could not tell the truth.
So I avoided any contact.
Now, coming back to this question.
I don't know.
At-- at that time, I didn't know.
Later on, I analyzed this, and maybe I have some-- some answer for that, why.
But it seems to me that first there was some kind of-- of strike of outrage.
Because when I was going from my place where I was living-- Podravska-- to Warsaw, where I was working, I had to pass along on the side of the ghetto, of the ghetto wall.
And when the-- this uprising was taking place, I have seen the-- the-- the-- the smokes from burning houses and the-- and the shooting and so on.
It made a-- a terrible impression on me.
It-- well, maybe my impression were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were different than other people because the people, other Polish people were quite-- well, to-- to some extent, indifferent.
And I-- that's what I'm saying.
It's very hard.
But [SIGHS] I felt some kind of pity, some kind of-- of sorrow that's happened to-- to those people.
I was not anti-Semite.
And I told you the-- the few episodes when I was treated like a Jew.
So somehow I had-- I was-- somehow I felt that I'm involved in that.
And at the very beginning, Liliana didn't-- well, she was at that time very pretty, well, relatively very pretty.
But I was not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in love from first sight.
I was not impressed about her beauty.
What I was impressed in her, that was her intellectual side.
I-- I was impressed about that she knew about movies, about the literature, and so on.
But [SIGHS] not, I would say, not like a boy is impressed by-- by a girl because of her beauty or something.
And my decision, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it seems to me, was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I wanted to do something different than the rest of people, of my people, Polish people.
I-- I had some urge to-- not to recompensate, but to give the example that I could be different.
I could-- the whole problem treated differently.
Not be that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] indifferent.
So that was the-- the-- my reaction.
My-- I-- I-- actually, you know, in life, many things, they happen spontaneous.
And there was not much of reasoning.
Because if I would reason, if I would think about, I would never get into this.
This is extremely dangerous situation.
It's incredibly dangerous situation.
But somehow, I felt that I have to-- to make up for that indifference of my people and even my family.
So that's the-- the answer.
At the liberation, we were still in the same-- well, not the same place.
Because this camp where we were put by this company [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was anyway, we did work at that time because it was Eastern holiday.
And, of course, everything was falling apart.
There was no-- no-- no work, and there was no decent work, because the-- the Russians were coming, OK?
And [SIGHS] in this camp, there was a-- one Frenchman, older, a little bit older than me.
And he was Italian origin.
And he was-- at that time, he knew a lady who had the house on the other side of-- there was such a-- a-- not a street, but rail-- not-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] would be highway, which was not finished.
So there was not finished highway.
And beyond that highway, there were small houses.
So from the camp, you could see only chimneys of these houses.
And he-- one of the ladies, owner of such a small house, asked him to come for the front coming to-- just to be in the house and to take care of the house.
So he met me and said, why do you don't-- both Liliana and you come with me, and we will sit there.
Because the-- the-- the camp was an open field.
So if there would be shooting and artillery, so they come, we definitely will be destroyed.
And there was wooden huts and so on.
So it's not safe.
And then being in this house, beyond this-- this unfinished highway, it will be safer because there will be some protection.
And we agree, and we took our small belongings, what we had, and we stay with this guy and this German woman who ran away from this place.
She went to Salzburg or somewhere there.
And we stayed, and we wait until the Russians came.
And, of course, there was our good luck.
And, of course, you know, we had to hear this shooting and seeing how the chimneys were razed off by-- by the artillery and so on.
And then one day-- and we were not only-- but also some of the other French colleagues of this guy were also.
But these colleagues, they were on the first floor, and we slept in the-- in the basement.
In the basement.
There was a-- a finished basement, and there were beds, and we sleep there with this Italian guy.
So one day, one of these French guys from the upstairs came out of the house because suddenly the whole night, there was no shooting.
He came out and came back, and he was crying, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] So the Russians are here.
And definitely, indeed, we have seen how the Russian soldiers were climbing this unfinished highway and descending on our side.
So when Liliana have seen that, she ran right away, and she embarrassed the first-- embarrassed-- embraced the first Russian soldier.
And she was screaming, you are my liberator, and so on.
[LAUGHS] So.
So that was when the Russian came.
Did you then return to Warsaw?
Yes.

Then we had to go to another camp because the Russian organized a camp for all foreigners because their policy was to investigate everybody because they thought that they were-- were German collaborators and so on and so on.
But they couldn't do it in Vienna, so their policy was to gather all the foreigners in Austria and ship them to the country from where they came.
So that organizer, they organized first the Yugoslav, from Yugoslavia people, and the next transport from Vienna, from Wiener Neustadt, there were the Polish people going to Warsaw.
And we came to Warsaw.
And of course, when we came to Warsaw, we came to my parents'.
And we came to my parents, well, that weekend, my sister was there.
But I didn't stay long because still there was some kind of hostility toward Liliana and me.
So I went to Gdansk, to Danzig, because I heard that in Danzig will be a technical-- the technical university would be-- be open, so I went there.
We came to Canada in 1961, February 17th.
Well, I had only three jobs because I'm now retired.
My first job, it was only-- I think it was two months.
It's RCA Limited in Montreal.
The second job, longer one because it was more than 10 years.
Was Canadian Marconi Company.
And the third and last job was Telesat Canada, here in Ottawa.
I was an electronics engineer and designer and telecommunication specialist.
And [INAUDIBLE] lately, with Telesat, I was considered a satellite specialist.
Whew.
My outlook?
Very pessimistic, first.
Why?
Because we have seen horrors all the time.
Horrors with the Jewish people, horrors with Polish people.
Can you imagine how many people were-- were killed and how many people perished in Auschwitz, Polish people?
And on the street?
It was dangerous even to-- to walk on streets because they were catching people for the forced labor.
And they were-- and they were doing-- well, the-- the Germans, they-- they practiced the-- the rule of so-called [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] commandeer [INAUDIBLE].
If somebody did so, so, so the whole community was responsible.
Well, how can we be responsible?
Some kind of, you know, common responsibility.
And they were doing these hostages, killing of hostages.
It was terrible.
It was-- So your outlook is pessimistic because of--
My outlook is-- was-- is pessimistic.
My private life suffered quite a lot because I lost-- you see, I am, I don't belong any-- to any-- to anybody.
I am not a-- a Polish, member of the Polish community because of the differences in-- in-- in a relationship between Poles and the Jews.
And I am not Jewish.
I don't belong to this community itself because I-- I have different religion, and well, it's hard for me to-- well.
So I am in limbo.
I am hanging in-- in between.
And specifically because we spent 12 years, and I started at university, Polish University, and in Poland.
And I suffered a lot that people didn't want to have any contact with me because I had Jewish wife.
They didn't like that, OK?
So I lost my friends, my colleagues.

I could not be open and sincere talking to people because if I would take a side of Jews and-- or a-- and-- and-- and express myself against these massacres, the Holocaust and so on, then right away, they say, oh, because you are Jew.
OK?
You know.
So a lot of conflicts.
I had a lot of-- even here in Canada.
Imagine, when I was working with-- with Canadian Marconi, I had no car at that time, in the very beginning.
And, of course, the people, they introduce me to one of Poles.
Apparently, there was a former Polish officer who was at Canadian Marconi working.
And he was living not far from that place what we were living in Tremont.
And he was coming to pick me up to go to work.
And when we were driving, we were driving through the lower of Tremont.
In the lower of Tremont, there is a lot of Jewish people living there.
And there the-- the-- the Jewish boys were playing on the street.
And he was swearing always to me, swearing on those boys that they are interfering with his driving.
And-- and once he said, too bad that Hitler was not in Canada that he could get rid of these Jewish people.
Terrible, right?
And interesting.
I did tell him that my wife is Jewish.
But one day-- but when I was working for Canadian-- for RCA, I was so naive because I didn't know that the people, Polish people living in Canada, they could be free, free from all this prejudice.
So.
And I-- there was another Polish colleague, engineer, working with the RCA.
And she-- and I-- I told him that my wife is Jewish, and we came, and I came with a Jewish wife.
Mhm.
